keyword: best herbal teas for upset stomach
intro_1: Upset stomach is a common problem that affects millions of people worldwide every year. It can negatively impact their quality of life by causing discomfort, nausea, and even vomiting. The main causes of upset stomach are food intolerances, infections, stress, and certain medications. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), it's estimated that 10% of the global population experiences gastrointestinal disorders every year, with an average of two episodes per person annually. This can lead to missed work, decreased productivity, and a decrease in overall well-being.
intro_2: Herbal tea is known to provide natural relief from an upset stomach. The various herbs and spices used in the blends have anti-inflammatory, digestive and soothing properties that can help calm the gut and alleviate discomfort. Chamomile, ginger, peppermint and fennel are some common herbs used in tea blends for this purpose. Drinking a cup of warm herbal tea can help reduce bloating, gas, and indigestion while promoting overall digestive health. It is a safe, natural and delicious way to manage an upset stomach.
title: 10 best herbal teas for upset stomach
intro: Herbal teas, especially those made from chamomile, ginger, peppermint, and licorice root, are known to soothe upset stomachs due to their anti-inflammatory and calming properties. These herbs can help reduce inflammation in the stomach lining, relax the smooth muscles of the intestines, and alleviate symptoms such as nausea, cramps, and bloating. Moreover, herbal teas promote fluid intake, which is essential for maintaining proper hydration and supporting overall digestive health. The warmth of the tea can also stimulate digestion, enhance blood flow to the digestive tract, and provide comfort during times of discomfort. Lastly, herbal teas are caffeine-free, making them a gentle and soothing option for those with sensitive stomachs.

remedies.remedy_desc: Chamomile tea helps with upset stomach because it contains chemical compounds like bisabolol, bisabolol oxides and matricin that have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties. These compounds help to reduce inflammation, relax the smooth muscles of the digestive tract, and relieve spasms, making it easier to pass stools and reduce stomach discomfort. Moreover, chamomile tea is also known to stimulate the production of bile in the liver, which aids in the digestion of fats and may help to prevent the formation of gas in the gut. Additionally, its calming effects can help reduce stress and anxiety, which are often linked to digestive problems. However, it's important to note that while chamomile tea can be helpful for occasional bouts of upset stomach, it's not a substitute for medical treatment, especially for chronic or severe digestive conditions.
remedy_parts: Flowers: Chamomile flowers are commonly used in medicinal tea for upset stomach due to their calming and anti-inflammatory properties.
Leaves: The leaves of chamomile contain therapeutic compounds that can help soothe an upset stomach and promote digestion.
Seeds: Chamomile seeds are rich in essential oils, which can help alleviate digestive discomfort and reduce stomach inflammation.
remedy_constituents: Azulene: Has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the stomach lining caused by upset stomach.
Bisabolol: Possesses anti-spasmodic properties that can help relax stomach muscles and alleviate symptoms of upset stomach such as cramping and pain.
Chamazulene: Has been shown to have gastroprotective effects, which can help protect the stomach lining from damage caused by acid and other irritants.
Apigenin: Has anti-inflammatory and relaxant properties that can help reduce inflammation and spasms in the digestive tract, providing relief from upset stomach.
Flavonoids: These plant compounds have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that can help soothe and protect the stomach lining, reducing symptoms of upset stomach.
Terpenoids: These compounds have anti-inflammatory, anti-spasmodic, and stomachic properties that can help alleviate symptoms of upset stomach such as cramping, pain, and inflammation.
Matricin: This compound is a precursor to chamazulene and has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the stomach and digestive tract.
remedy_recipe: In a small pot, pour 1 cup of water and bring it to a boil, which takes about 2-3 minutes.
Add 1 teaspoon of dried chamomile flowers or 1 chamomile tea bag to the pot.
Reduce heat to low, let it simmer for 5 minutes to allow the water to absorb the chamomile's properties.
Remove from heat, then strain the tea to separate the chamomile flowers or tea bag from the liquid.
Sip the tea while still warm, up to three times a day, as needed for soothing your upset stomach.

remedies.remedy_desc: Ginger tea helps with upset stomach because it contains gingerol, a compound that boasts impressive anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. This substance can help relax the intestines, allowing food to move through the stomach more easily and reducing the symptoms of an upset stomach. Moreover, ginger tea has been shown to stimulate the production of digestive enzymes, thereby facilitating the digestion process and preventing stomach discomfort. The soothing warmth of ginger tea can also help alleviate feelings of nausea and promote a sense of calm, further contributing to its gut-soothing abilities. Lastly, ginger tea is a caffeine-free beverage, making it a gentler alternative to other hot beverages that may exacerbate stomach issues, allowing for effective and comfortable stomach relief.
remedy_parts: Rhizomes: The rhizomes of the ginger plant are used to make medicinal tea for upset stomach due to their anti-inflammatory properties which can help reduce inflammation in the gastrointestinal tract.
Roots: Ginger roots are rich in volatile oils and phenol compounds, which can help relieve digestive problems such as nausea, vomiting, and stomach cramps.
Leaves: Although less commonly used than the rhizomes and roots, ginger leaves contain similar compounds that can help settle an upset stomach and soothe digestion.
Flowers: Ginger flowers contain potent antioxidants, and have been traditionally used in some cultures to treat various digestive issues, including upset stomach and indigestion.
Seeds: Ginger seeds have a pungent aroma and contain active compounds that may help stimulate digestion and alleviate symptoms of an upset stomach.
remedy_constituents: Gingerols: Gingerols have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce stomach irritation, alleviating upset stomach.
Shogaols: Shogaols can help relax the smooth muscle of the gastrointestinal tract, which can help relieve stomach cramping and upset stomach.
Paradols: Paradols have been shown to help reduce nausea, making them helpful in managing upset stomach.
Gingerdiols: Gingerdiols can help speed up gastric emptying, which can help alleviate symptoms of upset stomach.
Zingerone: Zingerone can help reduce inflammation in the stomach, providing relief from upset stomach.
remedy_recipe: Peel and grate 1 inch of fresh ginger; steep in 1 cup of boiling water for 5 minutes.
Squeeze juice of half a lemon into the ginger water.
Consume the tea while warm, up to three times a day, for soothing relief of an upset stomach.

remedies.remedy_desc: Peppermint tea helps with upset stomach because it contains menthol, a compound that relaxes the gastrointestinal muscles, which can help to reduce spasms and cramping. Additionally, peppermint oil has been shown to increase bile flow, helping to speed up digestion and alleviate symptoms of indigestion. Furthermore, the soothing warmth of the tea can help to provide immediate relief from discomfort, while the absence of caffeine makes it a gentle option for those with sensitive stomachs. The calming properties of peppermint tea may also help to reduce stress and anxiety, which can exacerbate digestive issues. Overall, peppermint tea's combination of muscle relaxation, digestion stimulation, warmth, and calming effects make it an effective natural remedy for upset stomachs.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Peppermint leaves are widely used in medicinal teas for upset stomachs due to their calming and soothing properties.
Flowers: The flowers of the peppermint plant contain healing compounds, making them a valuable addition to teas intended to alleviate stomach discomfort.
Seeds: Rich in antispasmodic properties, peppermint seeds are useful in tea formulations to help reduce stomach spasms and discomfort.
remedy_constituents: Menthol: Menthol has antispasmodic properties that help relax the gastrointestinal muscles, reducing upset stomach and abdominal pain.
Menthone: Menthone stimulates the production of bile, which aids in digestion and can alleviate symptoms of upset stomach.
Limonene: Limonene has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, providing relief for upset stomach.
Eugenol: Eugenol has analgesic properties that help alleviate pain and discomfort associated with upset stomach, and it also has antibacterial effects.
Rosmarinic acid: Rosmarinic acid has antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, which can help protect the stomach lining and reduce inflammation, providing relief from upset stomach.
remedy_recipe: In a teapot, combine 1 cup of boiling water with 1 teaspoon of crushed dried peppermint leaves.
Steep the peppermint leaves for 5-10 minutes to allow the active compounds to infuse.
Optional: Add a slice of ginger or a drop of honey for additional soothing properties and flavor.
Drink the tea while it's still warm, ideally between meals, for best results in soothing an upset stomach.

remedies.remedy_desc: Fennel tea helps with upset stomach because it contains volatile oils, such as anethole, which have antispasmodic properties that help relax the gastrointestinal tract, reducing stomach cramps and gas. The tea's anti-inflammatory properties, due to its rosmarinic acid content, can help soothe and reduce inflammation in the stomach lining. Furthermore, fennel's carminative effects promote the expulsion of excess gas from the digestive tract, alleviating bloating and discomfort. Additionally, fennel tea stimulates the production of gastric juices and bile, enhancing digestion and absorption, which can help alleviate symptoms of indigestion and nausea. Lastly, fennel's mild diuretic effect can help eliminate excess fluids and toxins from the body, supporting overall gastrointestinal health and function.
remedy_parts: Seeds: Fennel seeds are commonly used in medicinal tea due to their carminative properties, which helps to relieve digestive discomfort and reduce bloating.
Leaves: The leaves of the fennel plant have antispasmodic qualities, making them helpful in soothing an upset stomach by relaxing the gastrointestinal muscles.
Stems: Fennel stems contain volatile oils that can help stimulate the production of digestive enzymes, aiding in the digestion process and alleviating stomach issues.
Flowers: Fennel flowers are rich in antioxidants and have anti-inflammatory effects, which may help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract and promote a healthy stomach.
remedy_constituents: Anethole: This compound has anti-spasmodic properties, which helps relieve smooth muscle spasms in the gastrointestinal tract, thus alleviating upset stomach.
Fenchone: This compound has carminative properties, which help reduce bloating and gas, providing relief from upset stomach.
Estragole: This compound has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help reduce inflammation in the stomach lining and protect it from damage, thereby soothing an upset stomach.
Limonene: This compound has been shown to have gastroprotective effects, which means it can help protect the stomach lining from damage caused by acid, reducing the risk of an upset stomach.
Abuillion: This compound has antimicrobial properties, which can help eliminate harmful bacteria in the stomach that can cause upset stomach.
remedy_recipe: Chop: Slice 1 tablespoon of fresh fennel seeds or 1 teaspoon of fennel seeds powder.
Boil: Bring 1 cup of water to a boil in a teapot or a saucepan.
Steep: Add fennel seeds to boiling water, reduce heat to low, and let it steep for 5-10 minutes.
Strain: Strain the tea to remove the fennel seeds and pour it into a cup.
Serve: Drink the tea while warm, up to three times a day, between meals.

remedies.remedy_desc: Licorice tea helps with upset stomach because it contains active compounds, particularly glycyrrhizin, which can help decrease inflammation in the stomach lining, increasing mucus production and protecting the gut. This soothing effect on the gastrointestinal tract makes it effective in treating symptoms of gastric disorders, such as heartburn, acid reflux, and indigestion. Furthermore, licorice tea has been shown to stimulate the production of prostaglandins and mucin, substances that can help to regenerate and heal the stomach lining. It can also inhibit the growth of Helicobacter pylori, a bacterium that can cause stomach ulcers. However, it's important to note that excessive consumption of licorice tea can lead to side effects such as high blood pressure and potassium depletion, so it's recommended to consume it in moderation or use deglycyrrhizinated licorice (DGL) tea instead.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the licorice plant are commonly used in medicinal teas for upset stomachs due to their anti-inflammatory and soothing properties.
Rhizomes: The rhizomes of the licorice plant are used in teas to help treat upset stomachs because they contain glycyrrhizin, which can help balance stomach acidity.

Leaves: The leaves of the licorice plant are used in medicinal teas for upset stomachs as they can help stimulate the mucus membranes of the gastric tract, providing a protective barrier against stomach acid.

Flowers: Although not as commonly used as the roots, rhizomes or leaves, the flowers of the licorice plant can also be used in teas for upset stomachs due to their antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.
remedy_constituents: Glycyrrhizin: This triterpenoid saponin is the main active constituent in licorice, and has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and demulcent properties, which can help soothe and protect the mucous membranes of the stomach, reducing irritation and discomfort.

Flavonoids: These polyphenolic compounds have been found to have gastroprotective effects, possibly due to their antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce stomach acid production and inflammation.
Coumarins: These benzopyran derivatives have been shown to have spasmolytic effects, which can help relieve abdominal pain and cramping associated with upset stomach.
Volatile oils: The essential oils in licorice, including anethole and limonene, have carminative properties, which can help reduce gas and bloating in the stomach.

Phenolic acids: These aromatic compounds, including chlorogenic and caffeic acids, have been found to have anti-ulcer effects, possibly due to their antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help protect the stomach lining from damage.
remedy_recipe: In a teapot, combine 1 teaspoon of fennel seeds, 1 teaspoon of dried licorice root, and 1 cup of water.
Cover and let the mixture steep for 10-15 minutes to allow the ingredients to release their properties.
Strain the tea to remove the solid particles and ensure a smooth consistency.
Warm up 1 cup of milk (optional) and mix it with the strained tea for added comfort.
Sip the tea slowly, allowing it to coat your stomach and help alleviate your upset stomach.

remedies.remedy_desc: Turmeric tea helps with upset stomach because it contains curcumin, a compound with potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that aid in soothing the digestive tract and alleviating symptoms of upset stomach. The heating process involved in making turmeric tea enhances curcumin's bioavailability, allowing it to be better absorbed by the body and provide faster relief. Additionally, turmeric tea has been shown to stimulate the production of bile, which is essential for digesting fats and preventing stomach discomfort. Furthermore, its natural calming and warming effects can help relax the muscles in the gastrointestinal tract, reducing cramping and promoting normal bowel movements. Lastly, turmeric tea's soothing and mild flavor makes it a comforting and accessible natural remedy for those seeking relief from an upset stomach.
remedy_parts: Rhizomes: The underground stem of turmeric contains curcumin, a compound known to aid in digestion and provide relief from upset stomach.
Roots: The turmeric roots are rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory properties, making it an ideal part for soothing an upset stomach.
Leaves: Turmeric leaves have been used in traditional medicine to treat gastrointestinal issues, including upset stomach, due to their carminative properties.
remedy_constituents: Curcumin: This is the most active constituent in turmeric and has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce irritation and inflammation in the stomach lining, thereby reducing symptoms of an upset stomach.

Turmeric oil: This contains turmerone and other volatile compounds that can help stimulate the production of bile in the liver, aiding in digestion and reducing symptoms of upset stomach such as bloating and gas.
Curcuminoids: These are a group of antioxidants found in turmeric that can help protect the stomach lining from damage caused by free radicals, reducing inflammation and promoting digestive health.
Bisdemethoxycurcumin: This compound found in turmeric has been shown to have gastroprotective effects, reducing stomach acid production and preventing the formation of ulcers.
Demethoxycurcumin: This compound has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that can help soothe and heal the stomach lining, reducing symptoms of upset stomach.
remedy_recipe: In a saucepan, combine 1 cup of water and 1/2 teaspoon of grated fresh turmeric (or 1/4 teaspoon of dried turmeric) and bring to a boil.
Stir in 1 teaspoon of honey and 1/2 teaspoon of lemon juice, if desired.
Sip the tea while still warm, allowing it to soothe your upset stomach.

remedies.remedy_desc: Slippery elm tea helps with upset stomach because it contains mucilage, a substance that becomes a slick gel when mixed with water. This gel can coat and soothe the lining of the stomach and intestines, reducing irritation and inflammation. Additionally, slippery elm tea can help to protect the stomach from excess acid, further alleviating symptoms of upset stomach. Slippery elm may also help to speed up the healing process of any stomach ulcers or irritations, and it can help to regulate digestion, reducing symptoms of both diarrhea and constipation. Overall, slippery elm tea provides a natural and soothing relief for upset stomach, making it a popular choice for those looking for a more holistic approach to managing digestive issues.
remedy_parts: Bark: The inner bark of the slippery elm tree is used to make medicinal tea for upset stomach due to its high mucilage content, which helps to soothe and protect the digestive tract.

Leaves: The leaves of the slippery elm plant are used in teas for upset stomach because they contain antioxidants and may have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the gastrointestinal tract.

Buds: Slippery elm buds are a great addition to medicinal tea for upset stomach as they contain beneficial compounds with demulcent properties, which can help relieve symptoms such as indigestion, bloating, and diarrhea.

Seeds: Slippery elm seeds are rich in mucilage and can be used to make tea for upset stomach, providing a protective coating to the digestive tract and helping to alleviate symptoms like cramps, gas, and nausea.
remedy_constituents: Mucilage: Slippery elm contains a significant amount of mucilage, which becomes a slick, gel-like substance when mixed with water. This coats and protects the stomach lining, providing relief from irritation and discomfort.
Tannins: These compounds have astringent properties, helping to reduce inflammation and irritation in the stomach by constricting damaged tissues and promoting healing.
Starch: Slippery elm contains a high amount of starch, which can act as a demulcent, soothing irritated mucous membranes and helping to alleviate symptoms of an upset stomach.
Flavonoids: These antioxidants may help protect the stomach lining from damage caused by free radicals and inflammation, reducing the risk of stomach ulcers and other digestive issues.
Sterols: Sterols found in slippery elm have anti-inflammatory properties that can help soothe the digestive tract, providing relief from upset stomach symptoms such as pain, cramping, and bloating.

Essential oil: The essential oil present in slippery elm contains compounds like limonene, which have anti-inflammatory and calming effects on the digestive system, helping to reduce stomach irritation.

Vitamin A: Slippery elm contains vitamin A, which is essential for maintaining healthy mucous membranes in the digestive tract. This vitamin can help repair damaged tissues and promote the healing of ulcers and other stomach irritations.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, combine 1 cup of water and 1 teaspoon of dried slippery elm bark.
Bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 5-10 minutes.
Remove the pot from heat, and let the tea cool down for a few minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the bark, then drink it while warm.
Repeat as needed every 2-3 hours, up to 3 times a day.

remedies.remedy_desc: Marshmallow tea helps with upset stomach because it contains a substance called mucilage, which becomes slippery and thick when mixed with water, creating a protective coating on the lining of the stomach, soothing inflammation and reducing irritation. This soothing effect helps to alleviate symptoms of indigestion, heartburn, and acid reflux, making it an effective natural remedy for digestive discomfort. Additionally, marshmallow tea has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the gastrointestinal tract, promoting healthy digestion and easing digestive symptoms. Marshmallow tea is also a demulcent, which means it can help to relieve irritation and inflammation of the mucous membranes, further aiding in the relief of an upset stomach. Moreover, marshmallow tea has been shown to have a calming effect on the nervous system, which can help to reduce stress and anxiety that can exacerbate digestive problems, providing additional relief for an upset stomach.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the marshmallow plant are rich in mucilage, which can help to soothe and protect the digestive tract, making it an effective option for treating upset stomach.
Roots: The roots of the marshmallow plant are also high in mucilage, and have been traditionally used to treat digestive issues such as inflammation and irritation of the stomach lining.

Flowers: The flowers of the marshmallow plant contain antioxidants and have anti-inflammatory properties, making them useful for soothing an upset stomach and reducing inflammation in the digestive tract.
Stems: The stems of the marshmallow plant contain mucilage, which can help to coat and protect the stomach lining, providing relief from symptoms of an upset stomach.
remedy_constituents: Mucilage: The mucilage in marshmallow forms a soothing film over the lining of the stomach, which can help protect and ease irritation and inflammation.
Root extract: The root extract of marshmallow contains anti-inflammatory and antacid properties, which can help reduce stomach acid and soothe digestive discomfort.
Polysaccharides: Polysaccharides in marshmallow have a demulcent effect, which means they can help coat and protect the mucous membranes of the stomach, reducing irritation and promoting healing.
Flavonoids: Flavonoids in marshmallow have anti-spasmodic properties, which can help relax the smooth muscles of the digestive tract and reduce cramping and pain.
Pectin: Pectin in marshmallow can help absorb excess stomach acid, providing relief from symptoms of indigestion and acid reflux.
remedy_recipe: Boil 1 cup of water and steep 1 teaspoon of dried chamomile flowers for 10 minutes (recommended dosage for adults, adjust according to age and weight).
Add 2-3 marshmallows to the hot tea and stir until they are completely melted.
Let the marshmallow tea cool down to a drinkable temperature.
Sip the marshmallow tea slowly to soothe your upset stomach. Enjoy!

remedies.remedy_desc: Catnip tea helps with upset stomach because it contains nepetalactone, a compound that can help reduce intestinal spasms and inflammation, providing relief for various digestive issues. Additionally, catnip has antispasmodic properties that can soothe and calm the stomach muscles, reducing discomfort and pain. Furthermore, catnip tea can promote relaxation and stress reduction, which can indirectly contribute to easing an upset stomach as stress is often a trigger for digestive problems. Moreover, catnip has been shown to have carminative effects, helping to eliminate excess gas from the digestive tract and alleviating bloating and cramping. Lastly, catnip tea is a gentle and caffeine-free alternative to traditional teas, making it a suitable option for individuals with sensitive stomachs or digestive issues.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the catnip plant are commonly used in medicinal tea due to their soothing properties that can help alleviate stomach discomfort.
Flowers: The flowers of the catnip plant contain beneficial compounds that can help reduce inflammation in the stomach, making them a useful addition to medicinal tea.
Stems: While often overlooked, the stems of the catnip plant can also be used in medicinal tea for upset stomach, as they contain calming agents that can help relax the stomach muscles.
Buds: The buds of the catnip plant are rich in antioxidants that can help protect the stomach lining and reduce irritation, making them a valuable addition to medicinal tea.
remedy_constituents: Nepetalactone: This compound is responsible for the calming and sedative effects of catnip, which can help soothe an upset stomach.
Valepotriates: These iridoid glycosides have been shown to have antispasmodic properties, which can help relieve muscle spasms in the gastrointestinal tract.
Nepetalic acid: This organic acid has been found to have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce inflammation in the stomach lining.
Tannins: These polyphenolic compounds have astringent properties, which can help protect the mucous membranes of the gastrointestinal tract and reduce inflammation.
remedy_recipe: In a teapot, combine 1 teaspoon of crushed catnip leaves and 1 cup of boiling water; let it steep for 10 minutes.
Strain the mixture to remove the leaves, discarding the solid remains.
Warm up to 1 cup of milk (optional, for taste and added potential benefits); gradually add the strained catnip tea.
Stir in 1 teaspoon of honey or sugar, if desired, for added flavor.
Drink the catnip tea while warm, up to three times a day, to soothe an upset stomach. Adjust the catnip leaves quantity to taste and tolerance.

remedies.remedy_desc: Lemongrass tea helps with upset stomach because it has anti-inflammatory and calming properties that can assist in reducing inflammation in the digestive tract, thereby alleviating discomfort. The tea is also known to stimulate the production of digestive juices, which can improve nutrient absorption and promote regularity, leading to a more settled stomach. Furthermore, lemongrass tea contains citral, a compound with antimicrobial qualities that may help combat harmful bacteria in the gut, thus reducing the likelihood of stomach irritation. Additionally, lemongrass is recognized for its soothing aroma, which can help minimize stress and anxiety, both of which can contribute to digestive issues. Lastly, lemongrass tea is caffeine-free and gentle on the stomach, making it an ideal beverage for individuals seeking a natural remedy to alleviate symptoms of an upset stomach.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of lemongrass contain citral, a compound known for its antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, making it an effective natural remedy for upset stomach.
Rhyzomes: Lemongrass rhyzomes are rich in essential oils and phenolic compounds that help alleviate digestive discomfort, making them a key ingredient in medicinal tea for upset stomach.
Stems: Lemongrass stems have a calming effect on the stomach, reducing inflammation, and soothing stomach cramps, thus aiding in the preparation of medicinal tea.
remedy_constituents: Citral: The main component of lemongrass essential oil, citral has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which can help soothe an upset stomach by reducing inflammation and fighting off harmful bacteria.
Citral a: This compound, also a part of lemongrass essential oil, has been found to have antispasmodic effects, which can help relieve uncomfortable stomach spasms and cramping.
Geraniol: A type of alcohol found in lemongrass, geraniol has been shown to have gastroprotective effects, meaning it can help protect the stomach lining and reduce the risk of stomach ulcers.

Neral: Another component of lemongrass essential oil, neral has been found to have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help alleviate symptoms of an upset stomach by killing off harmful bacteria and reducing inflammation.

Limonene: Found in the leaves and stems of lemongrass, limonene has been shown to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, which can help soothe an upset stomach by reducing inflammation and oxidative stress.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, combine 4 cups of water and 2 stalks of lemongrass, bruised, and bring to a boil.
Reduce heat, let it simmer for 15 minutes to allow the water to absorb the lemongrass flavors.
Remove from heat, add 1 teaspoon of honey and stir well until fully dissolved.
Optionally, squeeze juice from half a lemon for added vitamin C.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the lemongrass, and enjoy the soothing tea while it's still warm.

Moreover, coriander tea stimulates the production of digestive enzymes, enhancing nutrient absorption and promoting a faster recovery. Lastly, coriander's high antioxidant content helps protect the stomach from oxidative stress and supports overall gut health, ensuring long-term digestive well-being.
